executiveSummary:
## Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

National Medicaid enrollment declined modestly in 2025, with 75.7 million enrollees across 51 states as of December 2025, representing the ongoing normalization following pandemic-era continuous enrollment provisions. Most major states experienced enrollment decreases, with Illinois showing the steepest decline at -6.5% year-over-year, followed by California at -5.6% and Florida at -4.0%. Texas maintained relative stability with a -2.2% enrollment change, totaling 4.08 million enrollees, positioning it as the third-largest Medicaid program nationally. North Carolina was the notable exception among large states, posting positive growth of +1.6%. Fiscal year 2024 expenditures totaled $215.9 billion nationally, with a per-enrollee average of $2,851, though significant state variation exists with Pennsylvania ($4,368 per enrollee) and Massachusetts ($4,185 per enrollee) showing substantially higher costs than the national average.

Texas demonstrates mixed performance compared to peer states, with several areas warranting attention. While the state's per-enrollee spending of $2,950 remains close to the national average and below expansion states like New York ($3,411) and Ohio ($3,522), its quality performance lags significantly. Texas scored 62 out of 100 on quality measures in 2024, ranking 30th nationally and placing it in the "moderate" performance tier—well below top performers like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80), and even trailing some non-expansion states. However, Texas leads in managed care adoption with 93.3% penetration compared to the 83.2% national average, suggesting strong infrastructure for coordinated care delivery. The state's $12.0 billion total Medicaid expenditure represents the fourth-largest program nationally, underscoring the substantial fiscal and health implications of performance improvements. Leadership should prioritize quality enhancement initiatives while leveraging the state's robust managed care platform to drive better outcomes and potentially reduce long-term costs.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.08 million enrollees, representing 5.4% of national enrollment despite serving 9% of the U.S. population. This enrollment gap reflects Texas's status as a non-expansion state, limiting adult eligibility and constraining coverage relative to its demographic scale. The state's -2.2% year-over-year enrollment decline is notably modest compared to peer large states like California (-5.6%) and Illinois (-6.5%), suggesting more stable unwinding from pandemic-era continuous enrollment policies. However, when compared to expansion peer North Carolina, which posted +1.6% growth, Texas's contraction highlights the structural limitations of its restrictive eligibility framework. At $2,950 per enrollee, Texas spending sits 3.5% above the national average of $2,851 but remains efficient relative to large-state peers like New York ($3,411) and Pennsylvania ($4,368).

Texas demonstrates operational strengths in managed care deployment with 93.3% penetration—significantly exceeding the 83.2% national average—indicating sophisticated care coordination infrastructure. However, quality performance remains a persistent challenge, with the state ranking 30th nationally at 62/100, trailing the national average of 65.6 and significantly behind top-performing large states. This moderate quality tier performance, while avoiding the bottom quintile occupied by neighboring southern states like Arkansas (54) and Oklahoma (51), suggests systemic care delivery gaps that warrant attention. Key areas to monitor include potential federal regulatory changes around interoperability and prior authorization that could impact Texas's managed care model, ongoing procurement activities that may signal program restructuring, and whether quality scores improve as post-pandemic enrollment stabilizes and the state can focus resources on care delivery rather than administrative churn.

alerts.title: PA per-enrollee spending 53% above national average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,368 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,851, indicating potential cost control issues.

alerts.title: FL per-enrollee spending 29% below national average
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,029 is substantially lower than the national average of $2,851, potentially indicating access or adequacy concerns.

alerts.title: Five states show critically low quality scores
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi have quality scores below 55, significantly underperforming the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: MA achieves top quality with high per-enrollee investment
alerts.description: Massachusetts demonstrates strong correlation between investment ($4,185 per enrollee) and outcomes (quality score 81), leading national performance.

alerts.title: FL shows concerning spending-quality disconnect
alerts.description: Florida combines the lowest per-enrollee spending ($2,029) with below-average quality outcomes, suggesting potential access or care adequacy issues.

executiveInsights.title: TX Medicaid quality score at risk tier - 30th nationally at 62/100
executiveInsights.summary: Texas ranks 30th out of 50 states with a quality score of 62/100, placing it in the moderate tier but only 2 points above the low performer threshold. This is 3.6 points below the national average of 65.6.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality performance directly impacts federal funding, contract renewals, and regulatory scrutiny. Being near the low performer threshold creates reputational and financial risk for both HHSC and Accenture.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement initiatives to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: TX per-enrollee costs $99 above national average despite efficiency focus
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,950 per enrollee compared to the national average of $2,851, representing a 3.5% premium. With 4.08M enrollees, this translates to approximately $404M in excess costs annually.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Cost efficiency is a key contract performance metric and higher per-enrollee spending signals potential operational inefficiencies that could trigger client scrutiny and impact contract terms.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost management strategies with HHSC finance team

executiveInsights.title: TX managed care penetration leads nation at 93.3% vs 83.2% average
executiveInsights.summary: Texas achieves 93.3% managed care penetration with 3.86M enrollees, significantly outperforming the national average of 83.2%. This represents strong operational execution in managed care transformation.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration demonstrates successful program implementation and positions Texas as a model for other states, creating potential expansion opportunities for Accenture's managed care services.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Leverage TX success story for national managed care expansion opportunities

executiveInsights.title: New interoperability and prior authorization regulations pending
executiveInsights.summary: Federal Register shows upcoming Medicare and Medicaid interoperability standards and prior authorization rule changes scheduled for implementation. These regulatory changes will require system modifications and compliance updates.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Regulatory changes create implementation work and potential contract modifications, representing both compliance requirements and revenue opportunities for additional services.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review regulatory timeline and flag compliance requirements to HHSC

executiveInsights.title: TX enrollment decline of 2.2% follows national disenrollment trend
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment decreased by 2.2% year-over-year to 4.08M, consistent with the broader national pattern of post-PHE unwinding. Most large states show similar 2-6% declines.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment declines affect revenue projections and may require operational adjustments, but Texas performance is better than larger states like California (-5.6%) and Illinois (-6.5%).
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review enrollment projections and operational capacity planning with HHSC

riskOpportunity.label: Quality measure gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks 30th nationally in quality scores at 62/100, creating pressure for improvement initiatives and potential CMS scrutiny.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive quality improvement roadmap targeting specific low-performing measures.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's 93.3% managed care penetration suggests upcoming MCO contract renewals worth billions in program administration.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor HHSC procurement portal and position for MCO systems modernization opportunities.

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline impact
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 2.2% YoY, potentially reducing program funding and administrative contracts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Assess contract revenue exposure and develop efficiency optimization proposals.

riskOpportunity.label: Interoperability compliance mandate
riskOpportunity.description: New federal interoperability and prior authorization standards create technology modernization requirements for state systems.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop FHIR-compliant solution offerings for Texas HHSC systems integration needs.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight intensification
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid portfolio oversight increases audit risk and compliance requirements for state contractors.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance frameworks and audit readiness across all Texas HHSC engagements.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's $2,950 per-enrollee cost is above national average, creating pressure for cost reduction and efficiency improvements.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose data analytics solutions to identify cost reduction opportunities in high-spend categories.

riskOpportunity.label: CHIP data collection expansion
riskOpportunity.description: Federal generic information collection activities for CHIP programs may require new reporting and data management capabilities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position data management and reporting solutions for expanded CHIP requirements.

riskOpportunity.label: State procurement digitization
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ESBD and procurement portal modernization creates opportunities for digital transformation consulting and implementation.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Engage HHSC leadership on procurement process optimization and vendor management improvements.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 4,083,939 Medicaid enrollees as of December 2025.

pulseMetrics.label: Enrollment Change
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid enrollment decreased 2.2% year-over-year in the latest reporting period.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $2,950 per Medicaid enrollee annually in fiscal year 2024.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 enrollees in managed care plans.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas ranks #30 of 50 states with a moderate quality score of 62 out of 100.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two active procurement opportunities are available through Texas HHSC and ESBD portals.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Seven regulatory and oversight items are currently active across HHS OIG and Federal Register.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific milestone dates are provided in the current data.
